blurb: Fragrant pink wild rose with red hips that hang on all winter.
seasons: Pink fragrant flowers May–Jul; red hips & canes persist through winter
wildlife: Xerces native/bumble bee value; hips feed birds & mammals
deer: Young spring leaves browsed — only somewhat resistant

care.hardiness: USDA zones 2–9 — among the hardiest wild roses in North America. Native from the plains to above 11,000 ft elevation, it shrugs off Front Range winters with no protection. Canes may die back to the snowline in a brutal winter but the root system is indestructible and regrows vigorously.
care.sun: Full sun for best flowering and densest growth. It tolerates light to moderate shade — woodland-edge plants bloom adequately — but open, sunny exposures give the most flowers and hips.
care.soil: Adaptable and tough: tolerates sandy to clay-loam, mildly acidic to alkaline soils (pH ~5.5–8.0), and Front Range alkaline clay-loam without amendment. Prefers moderately fertile, well-drained to moist soil. Avoid permanently waterlogged spots. No special soil prep is needed for a native planting; compost incorporation helps on very heavy clay.
care.water: Low to moderate once established — genuinely drought-tolerant as befits a plant native to dry foothills and prairie edges. Water deeply once a week during the first growing season to help roots establish. After that, watering every 2–3 weeks during summer heat is sufficient; on very dry years a monthly deep soak keeps it blooming and hip-bearing. Avoid frequent shallow watering and overhead irrigation, which promotes fungal disease.
care.spacing: Plant 4–6 ft apart if you want the shrubs to grow together into a hedge or wildlife thicket; 6–8 ft apart for individual specimens. Mature plants reach 3–6 ft tall (occasionally to 10 ft in sheltered spots) and spread wider via root suckers. Site it where spreading is welcome, or plan to manage the perimeter.
care.bloom: Fragrant, single, 5-petaled pink flowers (1–2 in across) open May through July on the Front Range — peak bloom is typically late May–June. Blooms form on the previous year's wood. Bright orange-red hips follow and persist on the canes well into winter, providing wildlife food long after the leaves drop.
care.feeding: Minimal. Wood's rose thrives in lean native soils without supplemental fertilizer. A light topdress of compost in spring is fine; avoid high-nitrogen inputs, which encourage lush soft growth susceptible to disease and reduce flower and hip production.
care.maintenance: Prune in late winter or early spring (February–March on the Front Range) before new growth breaks. Remove dead, damaged, and crossing canes at the base and thin congested older canes to improve air circulation — this is the key to disease prevention. Annual removal of a few of the oldest, woodiest canes keeps the shrub vigorous and blooming well; new canes from the base carry the best flowers. For a contained form, shorten long arching canes as needed; for a thicket or barrier, let it run and simply remove dead wood. Suckers at the colony margin can be spaded off each spring.
care.selfsow: Spreads aggressively by rhizomes and root suckers, forming dense prickly thickets over time. This is excellent for wildlife habitat, erosion control, and property-edge barriers; manage the perimeter with a sharp spade annually to keep it from colonizing adjacent areas.
care.troubles: Fairly robust compared to garden roses. Black spot (dark leaf spots, defoliation) and rust (orange pustules on leaf undersides) can appear in wet years — prevent both by watering at the base, pruning for airflow, and cleaning up fallen leaves. Aphids cluster on soft new growth in spring; a blast of water or insecticidal soap handles them. Spider mites may appear in hot, dry summers. Rose stem girdler (a native borer) occasionally kills individual canes — prune them out. Overall, far less disease-prone than hybrid garden roses.
care.harvest: Hips are ready to harvest after the first frost softens them, typically October–November. They are high in vitamin C and make good tea, jelly, and syrup — slice off the blossom and stem ends, remove the seeds and fibrous interior, and cook. Leave at least half the hips on the shrub for overwintering birds and small mammals.
care.companions: Chokecherry, mountain alder, red-twig dogwood, native willows, golden currant, rabbitbrush, and blue grama grass for a naturalistic Front Range hedgerow or wildlife planting. The prickly thicket it forms is excellent nesting habitat and complements open-meadow plantings of native grasses and prairie perennials.
